summary: Julia Evans catalogs the specific networking concepts and skills she's learned over roughly eight years, from basic HTTP requests to container networking. Rather than prescribing a learning path, she presents her knowledge as an accumulation of small, concrete skills picked up one at a time, mostly on the job. She lists tools she's found useful for networking tasks and describes her learning process as identifying small gaps, filling them, and repeating. The post argues that broad competence in networking comes from patient, incremental learning rather than any single course or resource.
key_points: Computer networking competence is built from dozens of discrete, concrete skills learned individually over years, not from a single learning resource
Practical tools like tcpdump, wireshark, curl, and dig are essential for hands-on networking understanding
The learning process is iterative: identify something small you don't know, figure it out, repeat
Most networking knowledge was acquired on the job when needed, not through formal study
Even with broad competence, there are always remaining mysteries (like wifi)
money_quotes: Like anything else, "computer networking" involves a large number of different concepts and skills and tools and I've learned them all one at a time.
"can reliably use tcpdump without reading the man page"
It's really fun to see how learning a bunch of tiny things adds up over time.
Today I feel like I can handle most things about computer networking that I run into in my job, and I don't feel like there are that many Big New Ideas about networking I don't know about.
I've found it really useful to take it one step at a time.
I've learned a lot of the things I know at work, and I think picking up new things when I need them has been fine.
key_insight: Broad networking competence comes not from mastering a textbook but from accumulating dozens of small, concrete skills over time through curiosity and on-the-job problem solving.
